text.title: Stakeholder Models — Salience and Responsibility
text.summary: Covers the Mendelow and Mitchell-Agle-Wood models for stakeholder classification and RACI/RASCI/DACI/RAPID for responsibility assignment.

rule.statement: Use Mendelow for engagement cadence (which quadrant maps to which channel), not for prioritization.
rule.rationale: Mendelow flattens politics to two axes and is silent on legitimacy and urgency; it is a channel selector, not a prioritization model.

antipattern.description: Treating Mendelow as the only classification model.
antipattern.reason: It does not capture urgency or legitimacy; a high-power actor without legitimacy is a bully, not a stakeholder — the correct response is escalation, not accommodation.

rule.statement: Apply Mitchell-Agle-Wood (power x legitimacy x urgency) to classify each stakeholder into one of 8 types: dormant, discretionary, demanding, dominant, dangerous, dependent, definitive, non-stakeholder.
rule.rationale: Salience tells you who matters and in what order; Mendelow tells you which channel to use. They are complementary, not substitutes.

rule.statement: Require cited evidence (quote, transcript line, org-chart entry) for every salience axis; return "unknown" if evidence is absent — never infer urgency from tone alone.
rule.rationale: LLM urgency inference from tone is a known failure mode; a regulatory deadline or contractual SLA is the only valid urgency signal.

antipattern.description: Classifying legitimacy using Anglo-American corporate norms only.
antipattern.reason: In high-context cultures (JP, KR, parts of EU) and in jurisdictions with statutory bodies (e.g., DE Betriebsrat), legitimacy flows through indirect networks the standard model does not capture.

section.title: Responsibility Models

rule.statement: Pick ONE responsibility model per program before any artifact is produced; document the choice and its rationale.
rule.rationale: RACI, RASCI, DACI, and RAPID use some of the same letters with different meanings; mixing models within a program makes every role assignment ambiguous.

list.item: RACI — steady-state operations: exactly one A per row, one or more R
RASCI — adds S (Support); use when "support" roles are common (data ops, infra)
DACI — time-boxed decisions with a single Driver and an Approver
RAPID — cross-functional decisions with split Input and Agreement roles

rule.statement: Enforce RACI integrity mechanically: exactly one Accountable per row, at least one Responsible, no R+A collapse to a single person. Lint in CI.
rule.rationale: Human-edited RACI tables drift toward multiple A entries and R-spam; a linter is cheaper than an audit finding.

section.reference: Bash script that fails if any RACI row has != 1 A or 0 R

rule.statement: Keep stakeholder, persona, and actor artifacts strictly separate: a stakeholder row never becomes a persona; an actor (UML) is a system-interaction role with no political weight; a persona has no decision authority.
rule.rationale: Conflating them breaks requirements traceability; a persona that inherits engagement cadence from a stakeholder produces meaningless engagement plans.

rule.statement: Maintain an explicit non-stakeholders list. Documenting exclusions prevents repeated re-litigation of who belongs on the register.
rule.rationale: Without a declared exclusion list, every new team member re-opens the same boundary debates.
